verses.text: Isaac is old and his eyes have grown dim. He calls Esau: go hunt me some venison and make me the food I love. Then I will bless thee before I die. The deathbed blessing in that culture was binding and final.

Isaac is old and his eyes have grown dim. He calls Esau: go hunt me some venison and make me the food I love. Then I will bless thee before I die. The deathbed blessing in that culture was binding and final.

Isaac is old and his eyes have grown dim. He calls Esau: go hunt me some venison and make me the food I love. Then I will bless thee before I die. The deathbed blessing in that culture was binding and final.

Isaac is old and his eyes have grown dim. He calls Esau: go hunt me some venison and make me the food I love. Then I will bless thee before I die. The deathbed blessing in that culture was binding and final.
Rebekah overhears. She has not forgotten what the LORD told her at conception — the elder shall serve the younger. She runs to Jacob: your father just sent your brother out for venison and blessing.
Rebekah overhears. She has not forgotten what the LORD told her at conception — the elder shall serve the younger. She runs to Jacob: your father just sent your brother out for venison and blessing.
Rebekah overhears. She has not forgotten what the LORD told her at conception — the elder shall serve the younger. She runs to Jacob: your father just sent your brother out for venison and blessing.
Listen to me, she says — exactly as I command. Go to the flock; bring me two young goats. I will cook them just the way your father loves. Take it in; receive the blessing meant for you.
Listen to me, she says — exactly as I command. Go to the flock; bring me two young goats. I will cook them just the way your father loves. Take it in; receive the blessing meant for you.
Listen to me, she says — exactly as I command. Go to the flock; bring me two young goats. I will cook them just the way your father loves. Take it in; receive the blessing meant for you.
Jacob protests: my brother is hairy, I am smooth. If my father touches me, he will know. I will bring on myself a curse, not a blessing. He fears the consequences, not the lying.
Jacob protests: my brother is hairy, I am smooth. If my father touches me, he will know. I will bring on myself a curse, not a blessing. He fears the consequences, not the lying.
Upon me be thy curse, my son, says Rebekah. Only obey my voice. A mother takes the blame in advance to keep her plan in motion.

Jacob brings the goats. Rebekah cooks. She dresses him in Esau's best clothes, wraps the goat-skins around his arms and neck, and puts the savoury meat in his hand. Every detail of the deception is staged.

Jacob brings the goats. Rebekah cooks. She dresses him in Esau's best clothes, wraps the goat-skins around his arms and neck, and puts the savoury meat in his hand. Every detail of the deception is staged.

Jacob brings the goats. Rebekah cooks. She dresses him in Esau's best clothes, wraps the goat-skins around his arms and neck, and puts the savoury meat in his hand. Every detail of the deception is staged.

Jacob brings the goats. Rebekah cooks. She dresses him in Esau's best clothes, wraps the goat-skins around his arms and neck, and puts the savoury meat in his hand. Every detail of the deception is staged.

Jacob goes in: my father. Here I am, says Isaac — who are you, my son? Jacob lies in two breaths: I am Esau, your firstborn. Eat my venison and bless me. The first lie is the hardest; after that they come faster.

Jacob goes in: my father. Here I am, says Isaac — who are you, my son? Jacob lies in two breaths: I am Esau, your firstborn. Eat my venison and bless me. The first lie is the hardest; after that they come faster.
How did you find it so quickly? Because the LORD thy God brought it to me, Jacob says. Even God's name is dragged into the lie. Sin gets bolder once it starts.
Isaac asks him to come close. He feels his arms: the voice is Jacob's voice, but the hands are Esau's. Isaac is suspicious — but the disguise is too thorough. He doesn't recognize him.
Isaac asks him to come close. He feels his arms: the voice is Jacob's voice, but the hands are Esau's. Isaac is suspicious — but the disguise is too thorough. He doesn't recognize him.
Isaac asks him to come close. He feels his arms: the voice is Jacob's voice, but the hands are Esau's. Isaac is suspicious — but the disguise is too thorough. He doesn't recognize him.
Art thou my very son Esau? I am. The third confirmation. Isaac eats the meat, drinks the wine. The blind father trusts what he can taste over what he can hear.
Art thou my very son Esau? I am. The third confirmation. Isaac eats the meat, drinks the wine. The blind father trusts what he can taste over what he can hear.

Come close, kiss me, my son. Isaac smells the field on his clothes. The smell of my son is as the smell of a field the LORD hath blessed. Now Isaac is ready to speak the blessing he had saved his whole life for.

Come close, kiss me, my son. Isaac smells the field on his clothes. The smell of my son is as the smell of a field the LORD hath blessed. Now Isaac is ready to speak the blessing he had saved his whole life for.

May God give you the dew of heaven and the fatness of the earth — plenty of grain and wine. Let nations serve you. Be lord over your brothers. Cursed is whoever curses you; blessed is whoever blesses you. The covenant blessing is poured out over the wrong son's head.

May God give you the dew of heaven and the fatness of the earth — plenty of grain and wine. Let nations serve you. Be lord over your brothers. Cursed is whoever curses you; blessed is whoever blesses you. The covenant blessing is poured out over the wrong son's head.
Jacob has barely left when Esau returns with his venison. He prepares the meat and brings it to his father: arise, my father, eat of my venison, that thy soul may bless me.
Jacob has barely left when Esau returns with his venison. He prepares the meat and brings it to his father: arise, my father, eat of my venison, that thy soul may bless me.

Who are you? Isaac asks. I am thy son, thy firstborn Esau. And Isaac trembles violently — the Hebrew suggests a man whose whole body knows what he has done. Who, then, was the one who came? And he shall be blessed.

Who are you? Isaac asks. I am thy son, thy firstborn Esau. And Isaac trembles violently — the Hebrew suggests a man whose whole body knows what he has done. Who, then, was the one who came? And he shall be blessed.
Esau cries with a great and exceeding bitter cry: bless me — even me also, O my father. Hebrews later cites this moment as the model of a regret that comes too late.

Thy brother came with subtilty and took thy blessing, Isaac says. Is he not rightly named Jacob? Esau cries. He has supplanted me twice — first my birthright, now my blessing. Hast thou not reserved a blessing for me? Isaac: I have made him your lord and given him grain and wine. What can I do for you now?

Thy brother came with subtilty and took thy blessing, Isaac says. Is he not rightly named Jacob? Esau cries. He has supplanted me twice — first my birthright, now my blessing. Hast thou not reserved a blessing for me? Isaac: I have made him your lord and given him grain and wine. What can I do for you now?

Thy brother came with subtilty and took thy blessing, Isaac says. Is he not rightly named Jacob? Esau cries. He has supplanted me twice — first my birthright, now my blessing. Hast thou not reserved a blessing for me? Isaac: I have made him your lord and given him grain and wine. What can I do for you now?

Hast thou but one blessing, my father? Bless me, even me also. And Esau lifts up his voice and weeps. Isaac answers him with what is left: your dwelling will be far from fatness; you will live by your sword and serve your brother. But when you grow restless, you will break his yoke from your neck.

Hast thou but one blessing, my father? Bless me, even me also. And Esau lifts up his voice and weeps. Isaac answers him with what is left: your dwelling will be far from fatness; you will live by your sword and serve your brother. But when you grow restless, you will break his yoke from your neck.

Hast thou but one blessing, my father? Bless me, even me also. And Esau lifts up his voice and weeps. Isaac answers him with what is left: your dwelling will be far from fatness; you will live by your sword and serve your brother. But when you grow restless, you will break his yoke from your neck.
Esau hates Jacob. He says in his heart: the days of mourning for my father are at hand — then I will kill my brother. The blessing wars become a manhunt.

Rebekah hears, and warns Jacob: flee to my brother Laban in Haran. Stay until your brother's fury turns away. Just a few days, she thinks. Why should I lose both of you in one day? It will be twenty years before she sees him again.

Rebekah hears, and warns Jacob: flee to my brother Laban in Haran. Stay until your brother's fury turns away. Just a few days, she thinks. Why should I lose both of you in one day? It will be twenty years before she sees him again.

Rebekah hears, and warns Jacob: flee to my brother Laban in Haran. Stay until your brother's fury turns away. Just a few days, she thinks. Why should I lose both of you in one day? It will be twenty years before she sees him again.

To Isaac she gives a different reason: I am weary of my life because of these Hittite daughters-in-law. If Jacob takes a Canaanite wife, what good is my life? She uses Esau's grief over his wives as cover. Truth and strategy braid together to the end.
